42.reason: [The error is that the code processes each line independently, but multi-line comments (e.g., /* ... */) span across lines. When the tokenizer encounters /* on one line, it skips to the end of the comment, but since the input is split by lines, the comment content on subsequent lines is not skipped—it is treated as regular code. This causes the comment text to be tokenized as identifiers and operators, producing extra tokens in the output.]

44.reason: [The tokenize method only handles multi-line comments within a single line. It does not track whether we are inside a multi-line comment across lines, so when the comment continues on the next line, the content is processed as normal tokens.]

102.reason: The loop exits when '*' is found at position i. This line increments i to skip the '*', but then the for-loop also increments i, causing the '/' of '*/' to be skipped as well if it's the next character. More critically, if the comment ends with '*/', the loop condition fails when i points to '*'. We need to consume both '*' and '/'. The current code consumes '*' here (i++), and the for loop consumes '/'. However, if the comment is at the end of the file or line, logic might be flawed. But the main issue is usually that we need to skip the closing '/'. Let's look closer. If input is "/* comment */", i starts at 0. i+=2 -> i=2. Loop runs until i points to '*'. Suppose '*' is at index 10. Loop stops. i=10. i++ -> i=11. For loop i++ -> i=12. Correctly skips '/'.
